text: The surgeon, docked on the left side, makes an incision approximately one centimeter away from the linea alba through the posterior rectus sheath, immediately entering the peritoneal cavity. The thin peritoneum tears easily, preventing the development of a preperitoneal space.

The surgeon operates intra-abdominally and pre-peritoneally, dissecting the ipsilateral edge of the peritoneum free to access the avascular plane outside the hernia sac. The defect is visible just beyond the sac edge.

The surgeon uses scissors like a hot blade to shave through the layers of the hernia sac, aiming to find a beautiful avascular plane. The sac is separated from the subcutaneous tissue or dermis by pressing, revealing multiple layers. The surgeon recruits the sac to patch the posterior gap, noting the ease of sac takedown and the potential to pull the anterior rectus sheath of the contralateral side into the dissection plane.

The surgeon carefully dissects to separate the hernia sac from the anterior fascia, ensuring the sac remains attached to the peritoneum on the contralateral side. The preperitoneal dissection continues below the umbilicus, with the posterior rectus sheath being opened near the arcuate line. The dissection stays parallel to the linea alba, revealing the rectus muscle extending laterally. The tissue becomes thin, leading to entry into the peritoneal cavity on the ipsilateral side, with similar conditions observed on the contralateral side.

The surgeon opens the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring it is away from the linea alba to avoid damaging the anterior rectus sheath. The preperitoneal space is expanded to expose the posterior rectus sheath, with visible bleeding from the ceiling being controlled to prevent hematoma formation. The peritoneum is peeled back, and the contralateral retrorectus space is developed further.

The surgeon displays the large hernia sac that was removed, revealing a significant hole in the posterior rectus sheath. The sac is used to patch the hole, effectively excluding the bowel from the retrorectus space. The sewing is complete, and the gap is fully covered.
